mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Czechia on?
mapQuestions.answer: Europe, right in the centre. Czechia is landlocked, surrounded by Germany, Austria, Slovakia and Poland 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Czechia?
mapQuestions.answer: Prague, on the Vltava river. About 1.3 million people live in Prague itself. It is one of the most-visited capital cities in Europe.

mapQuestions.question: What language does Czechia speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Czech, a Slavic language closely related to Slovak. Most younger Czechs and people in tourism also speak English.

mapQuestions.question: Why is Czechia sometimes called "the Czech Republic"?
mapQuestions.answer: Both names are official. "Czech Republic" was the long name. "Czechia" was made the short name in 2016 because it's simpler in conversation. Both are correct.

mapQuestions.question: What were Czechia's two halves called before?
mapQuestions.answer: Bohemia (the western half, including Prague) and Moravia (the eastern half). Together they made Czechia. From 1918 to 1992 the country was joined with Slovakia in "Czechoslovakia", which split peacefully in 1993.

foods.name: Svíčková
foods.description: Roast beef in a thick creamy root-vegetable sauce with a slice of lemon, a dollop of cranberry, a swirl of whipped cream, and bread dumplings. Czechia's national Sunday meal.

foods.name: Vepřo knedlo zelo
foods.description: Roast pork with bread dumplings and stewed sauerkraut. The classic Czech pub dinner. Hearty.

foods.name: Knedlíky (dumplings)
foods.description: Big fluffy bread dumplings, sliced into rounds, soaking up sauce from every Czech main dish. Like dense slices of soft bread.

foods.name: Trdelník
foods.description: A sweet hollow pastry-tube rolled in cinnamon-sugar, baked over hot coals on a wooden stick. Sold on every Prague street corner. Tourists love it, although Czechs will tell you it isn't actually that traditional.

foods.name: Smažený sýr
foods.description: "Fried cheese": a thick slab of cheese coated in breadcrumbs and deep-fried until the inside melts. Served with potato salad or chips. A Czech comfort classic.

foods.name: Medovník
foods.description: A tall sweet honey-cake of many thin layers stuck together with whipped cream and walnuts. The Czech celebration cake.

scavengerItems.label: Prague Castle on the hill above the river
Charles Bridge with its blackened statues
The Astronomical Clock in Prague's Old Town Square (animated figures appear every hour)
A Prague tram rattling around a corner
A wooden marionette puppet in a shop window
Cobblestone streets and red-tiled rooftops in any Czech old town
The Old-New Synagogue in Prague's Jewish Quarter (the oldest active synagogue in Europe)
Gothic spires and Baroque domes on the Prague skyline
A cyclist on the country roads between Prague and Brno
A deer in the Šumava forest or the Bohemian Switzerland sandstone park
Karlovy Vary spa town (a riverside village of yellow-pink mineral-water buildings)

geoMatches.description: The capital. A near-perfectly-preserved old city of Gothic spires, Baroque churches, cobbled lanes, Charles Bridge over the Vltava, and the famous Astronomical Clock.
A tiny medieval town in southern Bohemia, with a fairytale castle, narrow streets and the Vltava river curling around it.
A famous spa town in western Bohemia, where 13 natural hot mineral springs bubble up. People come from across Europe to drink the water from tiny porcelain spa cups.
A historic silver-mining town with the Sedlec Ossuary, a chapel decorated with thousands of human bones from medieval plague victims.
A national park of sandstone arches, deep gorges and pine forests on the German border. The biggest natural sandstone arch in Europe is here.
Czechia's second city, in Moravia. University town, modernist architecture, and the gateway to the Moravian wine country (Czechia's less-famous wine region).

senses.smell: Roast pork and dumplings in a Czech pub, cinnamon trdelník baking over coals, the dusty old-stone smell of Prague's castle, fresh mineral water in Karlovy Vary, pine forests in Bohemia…
senses.hear: Tram bells in Prague, "Dobrý den!" greetings as you enter shops, the wooden clack of marionette puppets, the chime of the Astronomical Clock at the top of the hour, the gentle gush of the Vltava…
senses.taste: Tangy creamy svíčková sauce, soft bread dumplings, hot cinnamon trdelník, sweet honey-walnut medovník, sour Bohemian dark bread…
senses.feel: Cobblestones underfoot, the warm steam of a Karlovy Vary spring rising on a cold morning, snowflakes on Prague rooftops in winter, soft Bohemian glass, the cool old-stone walls of Prague Castle…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why is Prague almost completely undamaged?
stories.body: Most of central Europe was hammered during World War II: Warsaw, Berlin, Dresden, Vienna, all heavily bombed or shelled. Prague, almost uniquely, came through nearly intact. The city was occupied by Nazi Germany but never seriously bombed by the Allies. As a result, Prague is one of the best-preserved medieval cities in Europe, with Gothic, Renaissance and Baroque architecture all surviving in the same square mile. The Old Town has been hardly redesigned since the 1700s. UNESCO put the whole centre of Prague on the World Heritage list in 1992. Walking through the old town feels like walking through a stage set, except everything is real.

stories.question: How does the Astronomical Clock work?
stories.body: Mounted on Prague's Old Town Hall is the world's oldest astronomical clock that still works, built in 1410. The clock face shows the time, the position of the sun and moon, the zodiac signs, and the seasons. Every hour, on the hour, a small show happens: a skeleton figure pulls a rope to ring a bell; small windows open and figures of the 12 apostles parade past; a Turkish figure shakes his head and a vain figure looks at himself in a mirror. The clock has been kept running for over 600 years, repaired and rebuilt several times, including after damage in the closing days of World War II. Tourists pack the square for the hourly show.

stories.question: What was the "Velvet Revolution"?
stories.body: In November 1989, after weeks of growing protests in Prague, Czechoslovakia ended 41 years of communist rule, peacefully, in only six weeks. There was no fighting, no civil war, no real violence. The whole change of government happened through huge candlelit demonstrations, students keys-jangling in city squares, and a series of negotiations. The dissident writer Václav Havel, who had spent years in prison for his essays criticising the regime, was elected president of the new democratic country a month later. The whole movement was nicknamed the "Velvet Revolution" because it was so smooth. Three years later, the country peacefully split into Czechia and Slovakia (the "Velvet Divorce"), again without a single shot fired.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Two huge political changes happened in this country, both completely peaceful. What makes a peaceful change possible instead of a violent one?

stories.question: Why do people drink the water in spa towns?
stories.body: Across western Czechia (Karlovy Vary, Mariánské Lázně, Františkovy Lázně) hot mineral-rich water bubbles up out of the ground from 13 different springs. Each spring tastes slightly different and is supposedly good for different problems: digestion, blood, joints, skin. Since the 1700s, European royalty, writers and composers (Goethe, Beethoven, Chopin, Russian Tsars) have come to "take the waters", drinking them slowly from special porcelain spa cups with a tube-shaped handle. Walking the colonnades of Karlovy Vary, you still see people drinking from those exact cups. The water IS slightly weird (warm, mineral, salty-bitter) and definitely an acquired taste.

stories.question: What is the Sedlec Ossuary?
stories.body: In a small town an hour east of Prague is a Gothic chapel called Sedlec Ossuary, decorated almost entirely with human bones, around 40,000 to 70,000 of them. The bones were collected during the Black Death and the Hussite Wars in the 1300s and 1400s, when so many people died that there was no space in the local graveyard. A monk, in the 1500s, started arranging the bones into decorations: chandeliers of skulls, coat-of-arms in vertebrae, pyramids of femurs. Today the ossuary is a strange, quiet, moving place that is part church, part memorial, part art installation. It is one of the most visited sites in Czechia.

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Czechia so far?
Have you tried "dobrý den" or "děkuji" yet?
Prague, small town, spa town, forest, which has felt the most "Czechia"?
How does Prague compare to other historic cities you have walked through?
Did you watch the Astronomical Clock chime? What happened?
A country changed government peacefully in six weeks in 1989. What can your country learn from that?

wordSearch.hint: Czech foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Red deer
animals.description: In the Šumava forest and Bohemian Switzerland. The biggest deer in Europe.

animals.name: Wild boar
animals.description: Across the Czech countryside, especially in autumn. Occasionally wandering into the outskirts of Prague.

animals.name: European beaver
animals.description: Returned to Czech rivers after almost going extinct. Look for chewed willow stumps along the Vltava.

animals.name: White-tailed eagle
animals.description: Czechia's biggest eagle, with a 2.5 m wingspan. Around lakes in southern Bohemia.

animals.name: Working farm dog
animals.description: Often a Czech wolfdog or a Bohemian shepherd, on small countryside farms.

animals.name: Brown bear
animals.description: Returning slowly to the Beskydy mountains on the Slovakian border.

animals.name: Eurasian lynx
animals.description: About 100 lynx live in Czech forests, mostly in the Šumava and Beskydy ranges. Almost impossible to spot.
